title: Apply: Methods of Constitutional Interpretation
description: Apply your your understanding of the methods of constitutional interpretation with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Civil Dialogue, The Constitution, and Constitutional Interpretation

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Bartkus v. Illinois* (1959).**

Some recent suggestions that the Constitution was in reality a [[☃ definition 2]] device for establishing a centralized government are not only without factual justification, but fly in the face of history. It has more accurately been shown that the men who wrote the Constitution, as well as the citizens of the member States of the Confederation, were fearful of the power of centralized government, and sought to limit its power. . . . Time has not lessened the concern of the Founders in devising a federal system which would likewise be a safeguard against [[☃ definition 1]] government.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Frankfurter use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *NLRB v. Canning* (2014).**

Historical practice over the past 200 years strongly favors the broader interpretation. The tradition of applying the Clause to pre-recess vacancies dates at least to President James Madison. . . . The [[☃ definition 1]] is that the President has consistently and frequently interpreted the Recess Appointments Clause to apply to vacancies that initially occur before, but continue to exist during, a recess of the Senate. The Senate as a body has not countered this practice for nearly three-quarters of a century, perhaps longer.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Breyer use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius* (2012).**

The Affordable Care Act is constitutional in part and unconstitutional in part. The individual 
mandate cannot be upheld as an exercise of Congress’s power under the Commerce Clause. That Clause authorizes Congress to regulate interstate commerce, not to order individuals to engage in it. In this case, however, it is reasonable to [[☃ definition 1]] what Congress has done as increasing taxes on those who have a certain amount of income, but choose to go without health insurance. Such legislation is within Congress’s power to tax.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Roberts use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Obergefell v. Hodges* (2015).**

Applying these established [[☃ definition 1]], the Court has long held the right to marry is protected by the Constitution. In *Loving v. Virginia* . . . which invalidated bans on interracial unions, a unanimous Court held marriage is "one of the vital personal rights essential to the orderly pursuit of happiness by free men." The Court reaffirmed that holding in *Zablocki v. Redhail* . . . which held the right to marry was burdened by a law prohibiting fathers who were behind on child support from marrying. The Court again applied this principle in *Turner v. Safley* . . . which held the right to marry was [[☃ definition 2]] by regulations limiting the privilege of prison inmates to marry. Over time and in other contexts, the Court has reiterated that the right to marry is fundamental under the Due Process Clause.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Kennedy use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Obergefell v. Hodges* (2015).**

Applying these established [[☃ definition 1]], the Court has long held the right to marry is protected by the Constitution. In *Loving v. Virginia* . . . which invalidated bans on interracial unions, a unanimous Court held marriage is "one of the vital personal rights essential to the orderly pursuit of happiness by free men." The Court reaffirmed that holding in *Zablocki v. Redhail* . . . which held the right to marry was burdened by a law prohibiting fathers who were behind on child support from marrying. The Court again applied this principle in *Turner v. Safley* . . . which held the right to marry was [[☃ definition 2]] by regulations limiting the privilege of prison inmates to marry. Over time and in other contexts, the Court has reiterated that the right to marry is fundamental under the Due Process Clause.


**Which statement best expresses Justice Kennedy’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Because the Court has held that the right to marry is a protected right, the Court reiterates that right in this case and applies it to same-sex marriages.
The Court does not agree with the precedent set in previous cases about the right to marry and has chosen to overturn those past decisions.
The right to marry is an enumerated right protected by the Fourteenth Amendment’s Due Process Clause.
There is no right to marry inscribed in the Constitution; therefore, the Court cannot rule on the constitutionality of laws banning marriage between certain classes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Dyer v. Kazuhisa Abe* (1956).**

Unfortunately, in an era where the concept of law over man is [[☃ definition 1]] as an achievement of our civilization, we find many legislators placing themselves above their fellow citizens in their duty to obey constitutional law. The courts have almost uniformly refused effective [[☃ definition 2]] to voters, mainly relying upon theories of separation of powers and the inability or reluctance of the judiciary to interfere with the legislature. But this is merely an easy way out of a difficult problem. To say something is delicate does not make it immune from the principles of justice. Any court action is important and delicate to the parties involved, as well as to the public, which has a civic interest in the effective functioning of justice. To allow ‘rotten boroughs’ to continue in open [[☃ definition 3]] of constitutional law, is a direct contradiction of the principles of justice. We are not asked to open the floodgates of the courts to the political problems of the legislature. We are requested to enforce the clear command of the fundamental law which the legislators have sworn to obey.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice McLaughlin use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Bostock v. Clayton County* (2020).**

Ours is a society of written laws. Judges are not free to overlook plain [[☃ definition 1]] commands on the strength of nothing more than suppositions about intentions or guesswork about expectations. In Title VII, Congress adopted broad language making it illegal for an employer to rely on an employee’s sex when deciding to fire that employee.  We do not hesitate to recognize today a necessary consequence of that legislative choice: An employer who fires an individual merely for being gay or transgender defies the law.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Gorsuch use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to, created by, or regulated by a statue or law

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *West Virginia State Board of Education v. Barnette* (1943).**

The case is made difficult not because the principles of its decision are obscure, but because the flag involved is our own. Nevertheless, we apply the limitations of the Constitution with no fear that freedom to be intellectually and spiritually diverse or even contrary will disintegrate the social organization. To believe that patriotism will not flourish if patriotic ceremonies are voluntary and spontaneous, instead of a compulsory routine, is to make an unflattering estimate of the appeal of our institutions to free minds. . . .

We think the action of the local authorities in compelling the flag salute and pledge transcends constitutional limitations on their power, and invades the sphere of intellect and spirit which it is the purpose of the First Amendment to our Constitution to reserve from all official control.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Jackson use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from Justice Frankfurter’s dissenting opinion in *West Virginia State Board of Education v. Barnette* (1943).**

It can never be emphasized too much that one's own opinion about the wisdom or evil of a law should be excluded altogether when one is doing one's duty on the bench. The only opinion of our own even looking in that direction that is material is our opinion whether legislators could, in reason, have enacted such a law.

**Based on the excerpt, Justice Frankfurter is opposed to which method of constitutional interpretation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *INS v. Chadra* (1983).**

We see therefore that the Framers were acutely conscious that the [[☃ definition 1]] requirement and the Presentment Clauses would serve essential constitutional functions. The President's participation in the legislative process was to protect the Executive Branch from Congress and to protect the whole people from [[☃ definition 2]] laws. The division of the Congress into two distinctive bodies assures that the legislative power would be exercised only after opportunity for full study and debate in separate settings. The President's [[☃ definition 3]] veto power, in turn, was limited by the power of two-thirds of both Houses of Congress to overrule a veto, thereby [[☃ definition 4]] final arbitrary action of one person. . . . It emerges clearly that the prescription for legislative action in [Article I, Sections 1 and 7] represents the Framers' decision that the legislative power of the Federal Government be exercised in accord with a single, finely wrought and exhaustively considered, procedure.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Justice Burger use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to a legislature with two chambers
not cautious or careful about the future

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Chief Justice Burger’s majority opinion in *Marsh v. Chambers* (1983).**

From colonial times through the founding of the Republic and ever since, the practice of legislative prayer has coexisted with the principles of [[☃ definition 1]] and religious freedom. . . . No more is Nebraska's practice of over a century, consistent with two centuries of national practice, to be cast aside. It can hardly be thought that, in the same week, Members of the First Congress voted to appoint and to pay a [[☃ definition 2]] for each House and also voted to approve the draft of the First Amendment for submission to the states, they intended the Establishment Clause of the Amendment to forbid what they had just declared acceptable. . . . This unique history leads us to accept the interpretation of the First Amendment draftsmen who saw no real threat to the Establishment Clause arising from a practice of prayer similar to that now challenged.

**What method of constitutional interpretation did Chief Justice Burger use in this opinion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: ending the practice of having a national religion
a religious official assigned to some institution

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s opinion in *United States v. Rahimi* (2024).**

English law had [[☃ definition 1]] not only [[☃ definition 2]] and [[☃ definition 3]] but also political opponents and [[☃ definition 4]] religious groups. By the time of the founding, however, state constitutions and the Second Amendment had largely eliminated governmental authority to disarm political opponents on this side of the Atlantic. . . . Such conduct was often addressed through ordinary criminal laws and civil actions, such as prohibitions on fighting or private suits against individuals who threatened others. . . . Our tradition of firearm regulation allows the government to disarm individuals who present a [[☃ definition 5]] threat to the physical safety of others.

**In the excerpt, Chief Justice Roberts draws on which method of constitutional interpretation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: robbers who steal from people traveling on roads
